nativeRange.habitat: Oceanic, pelagic; commonly found near the surface in open ocean and reef-associated zones at depths of 0–300 m

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on scraps from host sharks, parasites, small fishes, and invertebrates. In captivity, has been reported to accept frozen meaty foods and prepared marine flesh; may initially require live saltwater feeder shrimp to encourage feeding.

summary: The Pilotfish (Naucrates ductor) is a circumtropical open-ocean species best known for its commensal association with sharks and other large marine animals. It is widely regarded as suitable only for very large public aquarium exhibits, not home aquariums.
careNotes:
The Pilotfish (Naucrates ductor) is an oceanic, circumtropical species best known for its semi-obligate commensal relationship with sharks, rays, large bony fishes, and sea turtles — in the wild, individuals closely follow a host animal, feeding on scraps and parasites. Juveniles tend to associate with jellyfish and drifting seaweed before graduating to larger hosts. The species is widely regarded as a public-aquarium-only fish and is not typically seen in the home aquarium trade.

The Pilotfish grows to a common length of around 40 cm, with larger individuals recorded up to 70 cm. It is an active, fast-growing species, and a minimum tank volume of approximately 1,360 L (360 US gallons) has been cited as a baseline — even this may be marginal for long-term care. The aquarium should offer wide-open swimming space with minimal rockwork, allowing the fish to cruise freely.

The maximum recorded age for this species is approximately 3 years, suggesting a relatively short natural lifespan.

The Pilotfish is naturally schooling and tends to do best when kept with others of its own kind at a minimum of three individuals. However, it is regarded as aggressive toward smaller tankmates and will eat fish smaller than itself, so any compatible companions should be of similar or larger size. Small fish, slow-moving species, and invertebrates should be avoided in the same system.

Note: some trade listings use the name 'Pilotfish' for Gnathanodon speciosus (the Golden Trevally), which is a different species from Naucrates ductor. When sourcing or researching this fish, verifying the scientific name is advisable.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented. The Pilotfish is regarded as practically impossible to breed in home aquariums, and public aquaria have focused on display keeping rather than breeding programs.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: LiveAquaria's 'Pilotfish' product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/pilotfish) lists the scientific name Nathanodon speciosus (Gnathanodon speciosus / Golden Trevally), which is a different species from Naucrates ductor. The collection page was used as the primary source root per manifest; husbandry data was sourced from Quality Marine and FishBase which correctly identify Naucrates ductor.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed species ID 998 = Naucrates ductor (Linnaeus, 1758). Provided taxonomy, max size (70 cm), circumtropical distribution, temperature range (wild: 7.9–28.1°C), diet, and behavior notes.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Correctly identifies Naucrates ductor. Provided tank size (360 gal / ~1362 L), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), temperature (72–78°F = 22.2–25.6°C), pH (8.1–8.4), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate to difficult), and temperament (aggressive). Used as primary cross-verification source.

additional.name: Aquaryus
additional.notes: Provided temperature range 18–26°C and size 70 cm. pH data (5.9–7.8) and tank size (420 L) from this source were rejected as inconsistent with known saltwater parameters and other sources. Temperature values were averaged with Quality Marine.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding. This species is kept only in large public aquarium exhibits.
